stories.headline: Man arrested on suspicion of attempted murder after shooting scooter rider at downtown Denver intersection
stories.summary:
A 31-year-old Denver man, Anthony Davon Apodaca, was arrested April 4 on suspicion of attempted first-degree murder after allegedly shooting a scooter rider during a verbal altercation near 18th and Stout streets just before midnight on April 3. Police say Apodaca, riding in the front passenger seat of a white SUV, had been yelling at people at a nearby bus stop before confronting the victim at a red light and opening fire.

The victim told officers he was shot after responding calmly when Apodaca asked if he had a problem. A witness heard one gunshot before the SUV fled the scene. Officers located the vehicle within minutes, detained four occupants, and another passenger identified Apodaca as the shooter.

Apodaca was released on a $75,000 bail one day after his arrest, according to Denver County Court records. He is next scheduled to appear in court April 21 for an advisement hearing.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs driving school suspended for fraud; roughly 460 licenses to be revoked
stories.summary:
The Colorado Division of Motor Vehicles suspended the Academy School of Driving in Colorado Springs last week following a months-long investigation that uncovered widespread fraud beginning as early as March 2025. Investigators found that instructors marked answers for students on exams to guarantee passing scores, cut state-mandated 15-minute driving tests to under five minutes, and charged applicants illegal fees of $550 to $600 for guaranteed results.

The school also submitted falsified data on driving test routes and durations to the state DMV system and manipulated appointment availability to artificially inflate demand. Approximately 460 licenses issued through the school will be revoked, and employees involved face disciplinary action.

The DMV ordered the school to cease all testing operations pending a formal disciplinary hearing. Customers with paid or pending services must complete lessons and testing at another certified school. DMV Senior Director Electra Bustle said the agency will continue working with law enforcement to hold accountable those who attempt to subvert the driver licensing process.

stories.headline: Aurora officer stabbed in head with butcher knife near Cherry Creek State Park; suspect killed
stories.summary:
An Aurora police officer was stabbed in the head with a butcher knife near Cherry Creek State Park, according to authorities. The suspect was subsequently killed following the attack, though additional details about the circumstances of the suspect's death were not immediately available.

The incident represents a serious assault on law enforcement in one of the metro area's busier recreational corridors. Aurora police have not released the name of the injured officer or the suspect pending further investigation.

The officer's condition and the full sequence of events leading to the confrontation were not immediately disclosed. The incident remains under investigation.

stories.headline: Surge in habeas corpus filings reshapes immigration detention fight in Colorado federal courts
stories.summary:
Colorado has seen an unprecedented surge in habeas corpus petitions filed on behalf of detained immigrants in 2026, with more than 370 such cases filed in federal court in the first 100 days of the year — up from 104 in all of 2025 and just a dozen in 2024. The legal maneuver, once rare and technically complex, has become the primary avenue for immigrants seeking release from detentions that increasingly end only in deportation orders, as the Trump administration has sharply curtailed bail in immigration cases.

Denver immigration attorney Hans Meyer said his firm filed 60 habeas cases in the past six months after filing only six total in his first 19 years of practice. The crush of cases has been significant enough that attorneys from the U.S. Department of Homeland Security have stepped in to help federal prosecutors manage the workload, and U.S. Attorney Peter McNeilly has been personally involved.

The story is illustrated by the case of Manuel, a 23-year-old Colorado Springs man brought to the U.S. from Mexico at age 3, who was detained by ICE after a courthouse appearance for a dog bite case that was later dismissed. He was held at the Aurora immigration facility for two months before being released after his attorneys successfully filed a habeas petition. The trend reflects the growing legal battle over immigration detention policies in Colorado's federal courts.

stories.headline: Challenger Melat Kiros gains momentum against Rep. Diana DeGette in Denver Democratic primary
stories.summary:
Melat Kiros, a challenger in the Denver Democratic primary, is gaining traction against longtime incumbent Rep. Diana DeGette, who has represented Colorado's 1st Congressional District for nearly three decades. The contest reflects a broader national trend of progressive challengers targeting veteran Democratic House members in safe urban districts.

Details of Kiros's platform and the specific dynamics driving her momentum were not fully detailed in available reporting, but her rise signals increasing energy among Denver Democratic voters seeking newer representation. DeGette, first elected in 1996, is one of the longest-serving members of Colorado's congressional delegation.

The primary race is being watched as a bellwether for generational change within Denver's Democratic political establishment. A date for the primary has not been specified in available reports.

stories.headline: Nuggets secure No. 3 seed with win in San Antonio; will face Timberwolves in first round
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets locked up the No. 3 seed in the Western Conference playoffs with a 128-118 road victory over the San Antonio Spurs on Sunday, giving Denver home-court advantage heading into the postseason. Most of the team's rotation players rested for a second consecutive game, yet the Nuggets still secured the favorable seeding.

With the result confirmed, Denver will face the Minnesota Timberwolves in the first round of the NBA playoffs. Game 1 is scheduled but the specific date was not immediately confirmed in available reporting. The Nuggets and Timberwolves are familiar postseason opponents, having met in previous playoff rounds.

The regular-season finale caps what Denver coach David Adelman has called a rollercoaster season, one that included 45 games decided by single digits and nine overtime contests. Nikola Jokic and the Nuggets are entering their 17th playoff series in an eight-year stretch.

stories.headline: Avalanche coach Jared Bednar will not travel with team for final two road games of regular season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche announced Sunday that head coach Jared Bednar will not accompany the team for its final two road games of the regular season in Edmonton. The organization did not immediately disclose the reason for his absence.

The news comes as the Avalanche are focused on staying healthy heading into the postseason, with the outcome of the season's final regular-season games carrying limited stakes for the team's standing. The club did not indicate whether an assistant coach would handle bench duties in Bednar's place.

Bednar has been the Avalanche's head coach since 2016 and led the team to its Stanley Cup championship in 2022. His absence for the road trip is considered a precautionary measure as the team prepares for the playoffs.

stories.headline: Colorado Rockies face mounting on-field struggles as Monfort ownership dispute comes to light
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies are confronting both persistent on-field failures and an emerging account of dysfunction within the Monfort family ownership structure, according to reporting from the Denver Gazette. The team has made losing 100 games a recurring habit in recent seasons, and a long-hidden dispute between brothers Dick and Charlie Monfort over the franchise's future has now come to the surface.

The report describes the ownership conflict as a reflection of broader organizational dysfunction that has contributed to the team's decline. The Rockies have an off day Monday before traveling to Houston to face the Astros on Tuesday.

The franchise's struggles come amid a period of significant change, including the recent acquisition of a 40% stake in the team by the Broncos ownership group. The full extent of the Monfort dispute and its implications for the team's direction have not yet been disclosed.

stories.headline: Red Flag Warning, high fire danger expected Monday across plains south and east of Denver
stories.summary:
A Red Flag Warning is in effect south and east of Denver on Monday as warm temperatures, very low humidity, and gusty afternoon winds combine to create dangerous fire conditions across the region. Meteorologists warn that any fire ignited under these conditions could spread rapidly, and residents are urged to exercise extra caution.

Sunday brought temperatures into the 70s and 80s across the Front Range and eastern plains, a warm pattern expected to persist into Monday. The low humidity is particularly acute across open plains and southeastern areas of the state.

Relief is anticipated by late Monday night into Tuesday as the next weather system moves in, bringing mountain snow and rain showers to the plains, along with cooler temperatures. Another system is possible toward the end of the week, continuing an active spring weather pattern for the region.

stories.headline: Evacuation order lifted after wildfire breaks out northeast of Colorado Springs
stories.summary:
Mandatory evacuation orders were lifted Sunday evening for a wildfire that ignited northeast of Colorado Springs, according to the El Paso County Sheriff's Office. The evacuation order had covered a half-mile radius around the intersection of Curtis and Garrett roads in El Paso County.

Additional details about the fire, including its total acreage burned and the cause of ignition, were not immediately available Sunday evening. The lifting of the evacuation order indicates the immediate threat to nearby residents has passed.

The fire occurred amid elevated fire weather conditions across the region, with warm temperatures, low humidity, and gusty winds affecting much of eastern Colorado over the weekend.

stories.headline: Space Symposium returns to Colorado Springs with focus on Artemis moon program and deep space exploration
stories.summary:
Global space industry leaders, government officials, and national space agencies are gathered in Colorado Springs this week for the 41st annual Space Symposium, organized by the Colorado Springs-based Space Foundation. The event is centered on the Artemis program and the future of sustained human presence on the moon, following the successful return of Artemis II astronauts who traveled farther from Earth than any humans in history.

NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman is scheduled to speak at the symposium Tuesday. The mission's success relied heavily on contributions from approximately 14,000 Coloradans, with state companies building the crew capsule, its computers, and key rocket components. Roughly 11,000 attendees and 402 exhibitors from around the world are expected, including delegations from Japan, France, Italy, and the United Arab Emirates.

The symposium highlights Colorado's significant role in the national and global space economy. Organizers note that the global space economy is on track to cross major financial thresholds in coming years, with deep space exploration and a future crewed Mars mission as long-term goals of NASA and its international partners.

stories.headline: Aims Community College fast-tracks air traffic controllers amid national shortage
stories.summary:
Aims Community College in Windsor has become one of the first institutions in the country cleared by the Federal Aviation Administration to offer an enhanced air traffic control training curriculum, allowing graduates to bypass the traditional course at the FAA Academy in Oklahoma City and move directly to airport facilities for final training. The program addresses a nationwide hiring crisis in which the FAA estimates it will need to fill nearly 7,000 positions over the next three years.

The two-year associate degree costs in-state students roughly $13,000 to $17,000 in tuition and lab fees, leading to a career with starting pay above $50,000 as a trainee and a median salary exceeding $144,000. Aims has approximately 37 students enrolled in its standard program, which is capped at 50 due to equipment limitations in the lab. Metropolitan State University of Denver also offers an FAA-approved basic air traffic curriculum.

The first student expected to graduate from the enhanced program is Aiden Rowe, a 20-year-old from Cañon City who discovered his interest in air traffic control during a seventh-grade career research project. Program faculty lead Patti Phillips, a retired 30-year air traffic controller, said many potential students remain unaware the career pathway exists.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: Men don't care what's on TV. They only care what else is on TV.

topStory.headline: Trump Orders Naval Blockade of Strait of Hormuz After Iran Nuclear Talks Collapse in Pakistan
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump announced Sunday that the United States Navy would begin blockading the Strait of Hormuz following the collapse of 21-hour peace negotiations between American and Iranian delegations in Islamabad, Pakistan. U.S. Central Command said the blockade would take effect Monday at 10 a.m. ET, with warships ordered to intercept vessels entering or leaving the strategic waterway. Oil prices surged in early trading after the announcement, with U.S. crude rising 8 percent to $104.24 a barrel and Brent crude climbing 7 percent to $102.29.

Vice President JD Vance, who led the U.S. delegation and said he spoke with Trump at least six times during the talks, stated that Iran had refused to commit to abandoning its nuclear weapons program, which he described as the central sticking point. Iranian state media attributed the breakdown to what it called "excessive demands" by the American side. Trump, speaking to reporters at Joint Base Andrews after returning from Air Force One, said he was not pressing for another round of talks and described what he characterized as significant military gains against Iran, saying "their military is gone" and their missile manufacturing capability was "largely defeated."

Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned that any warships approaching the strait to enforce a blockade would be considered a breach of the current ceasefire and would be met with a strong response, insisting the strait remained under Iranian control. Two U.S. destroyers had crossed the strait without incident on Saturday as part of what the U.S. military described as a mine-clearance mission, though Iranian media reported the vessels were threatened upon departure. Trump also threatened to strike Iranian civilian infrastructure, including water treatment facilities, power plants, and bridges, if Tehran did not agree to give up its nuclear program.

Israel expressed support for the blockade, with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu saying in a video address to his cabinet that Israel backed what he called Trump's "firm stance." Netanyahu said Vance called him from his plane returning from Pakistan and provided a detailed briefing on the negotiations, with the vice president emphasizing that full removal of enriched nuclear material and a halt to further enrichment were the core demands. Netanyahu dismissed reports of any disconnect between Washington and Jerusalem as "the complete opposite" of the truth.

The Strait of Hormuz is one of the world's most critical oil shipping chokepoints, and the blockade announcement carries significant implications for global energy markets and U.S. relations with major oil importers. Approximately 100 tankers have transited the strait since the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran, with many carrying Iranian oil to China and India, paying up to $2 million per passage. Trump also drew a sharp public rebuke from Pope Leo XIV, whom he criticized as "weak on crime" and "terrible for foreign policy" after the pope condemned U.S. threats to strike Iranian civilian infrastructure and called for a negotiated peace.
